---
title: "Polyglot Analysis: Stata + Python"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
format: html
engine: knitr
---

## Overview

This document demonstrates a **Stata + Python polyglot** Quarto workflow. Stata
handles data loading and statistical modelling; Python handles visualisation via
`matplotlib` / `seaborn`. Data is shared through a CSV file written by Stata and
read by Python. Python chunks are executed by knitr via the `reticulate` R package,
which means R must be installed even though no R analysis is performed here.

> **Prerequisites**
> - R ≥ 4.0 with packages: `knitr`, `reticulate`
>   (`install.packages(c("knitr", "reticulate"))`)
> - Python ≥ 3.9 with packages: `pandas`, `matplotlib`, `seaborn`
>   (`pip install pandas matplotlib seaborn`)
> - Stata 17 or later
> - Update `engine.path` and `python` path below to match your installation

---

## Setup

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(reticulate)

# Point reticulate at the Python you want to use.
# Remove or adjust if you rely on RETICULATE_PYTHON env var or a project-level
# .python-version / renv Python configuration.
# use_python("/path/to/your/python", required = TRUE)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo    = TRUE,
  comment = "",
  engine.path = list(
    stata = "/path/to/stata"
    # Linux / macOS : "/usr/local/stata18/stata-mp"
    # Windows       : "C:/Program Files/Stata18/StataMP-64.exe"
  )
)
```

---

## Step 1: Load data (Stata)

```{stata load}
sysuse auto, clear
describe
summarize price mpg weight foreign
```

## Step 2: Statistical model (Stata)

```{stata regression}
regress price mpg weight i.foreign
```

## Step 3: Export to CSV (Stata)

Python will read this file in the next section.

```{stata export}
export delimited auto_temp.csv, replace
```

## Step 4: Read and inspect in Python

```{python read}
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("auto_temp.csv")
df["origin"] = df["foreign"].map({0: "Domestic", 1: "Foreign"})

print(df.shape)
print(df[["price", "mpg", "weight", "origin"]].describe())
```

## Step 5: Visualise in Python

```{python scatter, fig.cap="Price versus mileage, coloured by car origin"}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
sns.scatterplot(
    data    = df,
    x       = "mpg",
    y       = "price",
    hue     = "origin",
    palette = {"Domestic": "#1f77b4", "Foreign": "#d62728"},
    s       = 60,
    alpha   = 0.8,
    ax      = ax,
)
sns.regplot(
    data      = df[df["origin"] == "Domestic"],
    x         = "mpg",
    y         = "price",
    scatter   = False,
    color     = "#1f77b4",
    line_kws  = {"linewidth": 1},
    ax        = ax,
)
sns.regplot(
    data      = df[df["origin"] == "Foreign"],
    x         = "mpg",
    y         = "price",
    scatter   = False,
    color     = "#d62728",
    line_kws  = {"linewidth": 1},
    ax        = ax,
)
ax.set_title("Price vs. Mileage by Car Origin")
ax.set_xlabel("Mileage (mpg)")
ax.set_ylabel("Price (USD)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Step 6: Summary statistics in Python

```{python summary}
summary = (
    df.groupby("origin")[["price", "mpg", "weight"]]
    .agg(["mean", "std"])
    .round(1)
)
print(summary.to_string())
```

---

## Cleanup

```{python cleanup, include=FALSE}
import os
os.remove("auto_temp.csv")
```

---

## Alternative: Jupyter + nbstata (no R required)

If you want Python + Stata without R, consider a two-document approach:

1. A **Stata-only** `.qmd` using `jupyter: nbstata` that produces intermediate
   output files.
2. A **Python-only** `.qmd` using `jupyter: python3` that reads those files.

Quarto's [include shortcode](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/includes.html)
can then stitch them into a single rendered report.

---

## Positron IDE Note

When opening this file in Positron with the `positron-stata` extension active,
Stata cells execute in the running Stata session and Python cells execute in the
running Python session. No `engine.path` or `reticulate` configuration is needed
for the interactive preview path.
